front: Figure: this general conquered most of Europe as Emperor of the French before being exiled after his invasion of Russia and his defeat at the Battle of Waterloo
Figure: was repeatedly painted by Jacques-Louis David -- at his coronation, in his study, and crossing the Alps
Work: this 1812 Russian invasion serves as the historical backdrop of this Leo Tolstoy novel
Figure: born on Corsica while it was under French control, and as a young man led riots against French troops there
work: Early Life, Corsica & Rise to Power

front: Event: as a young artillery officer, commanded the Republican artillery that helped recapture this port city from Royalist and British forces in 1793
work: Early Life, Corsica & Rise to Power

front: Event: overthrew the Directory in this 1799 coup, becoming First Consul with the help of his brother, President of the Council of Five Hundred
work: The Directory & the Coup of 18 Brumaire

front: Event: as a general under the Directory, dissolved the leftist Pantheon Club, tied to Francois-Noel Babeuf's Conspiracy of the Equals
work: The Directory & the Coup of 18 Brumaire

front: Event: when accused of violating the Constitution during the 1799 coup, claimed his opponents had already destroyed it, prompting cries of "Caesar-Cromwell-Tyrant!"
work: The Directory & the Coup of 18 Brumaire

front: Event: in the aftermath of the 18 Brumaire coup, Roger Ducos was elevated in position and this new founding document was created
work: The Directory & the Coup of 18 Brumaire

front: War: invaded Egypt in 1798 to disrupt British colonial interests in South Asia, defeating the Mamluks at the Battle of the Pyramids and capturing Malta en route
Event: this British admiral's victory over Napoleon's fleet at the Battle of the Nile forced him to evacuate his Egyptian invasion
Event: Napoleon's siege of this Levantine city was defeated by the resistance of Jazzar Pasha (aided by the blind Jew Haim Farhi) and British artillery shipments credited to William Sidney Smith
Event: at this city, Napoleon executed thousands of Jazzar Pasha's mostly-Albanian troops as prisoners of war
Event: issued a proclamation to the Egyptian people affirming his devotion to Islam, printed on Egypt's first Arabic printing press and critiqued by the chronicler al-Jabarti
Event: the Egyptian campaign ended when this subordinate of Napoleon surrendered in the Capitulation of Alexandria
Law: this unified civil code replaced France's decentralized feudal-era coutumes and purged the opposition Tribunate of figures like Benjamin Constant
work: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms

front: Figure: said the Napoleonic Code would be remembered longer than his "40 battles," such as Ulm and Jena
work: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms

front: Law: the opening section of this document, named for "persons," forbade women from entering into contracts or from divorce by mutual consent
work: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms

front: Event: convened this assembly of European Jewish communities, chaired by Rabbi David Sinzheim, whom he gave the rank of "nasi"
work: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms

front: Law: restored the legal existence of the French nobility (the Second Estate) with an 1804 law, ending an abolition dating to 1789
work: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms

front: Event: reconciled with this pope through the 1801 Concordat, retaining the right to nominate bishops
work: Consolidation of Power: The Napoleonic Code & Domestic Reforms

front: Event: crowned himself Emperor of the French in a December 1804 ceremony at Notre Dame, breaking with the tradition of holding it at this city
Event: before his coronation, was hastily given a religious marriage to Josephine by his uncle, this cardinal, so she could participate
Event: his coronation was delayed from its originally planned date, the anniversary of this earlier coup
War: won this 1805 battle, defeating the combined armies of Alexander I of Russia and Francis I of Austria in the War of the Third Coalition
work: Wars of the Empire: Austerlitz, Jena & Tilsit

front: Event: completed this maneuver, encircling General Karl Mack's Austrian army and forcing its surrender without a major battle
work: Wars of the Empire: Austerlitz, Jena & Tilsit

front: War: in his 1806 campaign against Prussia, marched through the Franconian Forest to the Saale River, catching a Prussian rearguard commanded by Hohenlohe
work: Wars of the Empire: Austerlitz, Jena & Tilsit

front: Event: signed these 1807 treaties with Tsar Alexander I on a raft on the Neman River, creating the Confederation of the Rhine
work: Wars of the Empire: Austerlitz, Jena & Tilsit

front: Event: signed a never-enacted treaty with Fath Ali Shah of this Qajar-ruled country, and sent a letter proposing alliance to a ruler that was intercepted at Muscat
work: Wars of the Empire: Austerlitz, Jena & Tilsit

front: Event: began this embargo of the United Kingdom ("a nation of shopkeepers") by much of Europe, launched with the Berlin and Milan Decrees
work: The Continental System & the Peninsular War

front: Event: non-compliance with the Continental System forced this Portuguese royal house to flee with their court to Brazil
work: The Continental System & the Peninsular War

front: War: invaded Portugal and Spain to enforce the Continental System, opening this war that first coined the term "guerrilla warfare"
work: The Continental System & the Peninsular War

front: Figure: this marshal was forced to retreat from Portugal after failing to breach the secretly-built Lines of Torres Vedras defending Lisbon
work: The Continental System & the Peninsular War

front: Event: the Duke of Wellington's decisive victory at this battle effectively ended the Peninsular War
work: The Continental System & the Peninsular War

front: Figure: Napoleon installed this brother as puppet King of Spain during the Peninsular War, governing under the 1808 Bayonne Statute
work: The Continental System & the Peninsular War

front: War: over 600,000 soldiers began this 1812 campaign, but only around 27,000 returned, with many dying of cold during the retreat
War: won a pyrrhic victory over Mikhail Kutuzov at this battle, where attacks on the Bagration fleches and Raevsky Redoubt caused heavy losses, before occupying a scorched Moscow
Figure: struggled to supply the Grande Armee in Russia, encouraging soldiers to forage ("maraude") for food and is commonly credited with the quote "an army marches on its stomach"
Event: crossing this river became a byword for catastrophe during the retreat from Russia, visualized in Charles Minard's pioneering flow-map diagram
Event: after being checked at Maloyaroslavets during the retreat from Russia, abandoned his troops to rush home and suppress this conspiracy
Event: won this campaign of five victories in six days, including one at Vauchamps, but failed to stop the Senat Conservateur from removing him from power
Event: after his final defeat, was exiled to this remote South Atlantic island, whose Longwood House is now a museum
Event: nearly two decades after his 1821 death, his remains were exhumed and transported to Paris for a state funeral concluding at this site in December 1840
Event: a pastor's son named Friedrich Staps attempted to assassinate Napoleon in this city in 1809, later refusing pardon and declaring "To kill you would not have been a crime, but a duty!"
Event: survived a bombing attempt using this type of device on the Rue Saint-Nicaise
Work: this Jacques-Louis David painting depicts Napoleon crowning his wife Josephine at Notre Dame while Pope Pius VII, who probably did little, bestows a blessing
Work: The Coronation of Napoleon inaccurately includes this figure, Napoleon's mother, in its background, despite her absence from the actual event
Figure: upon first seeing The Coronation of Napoleon, he reportedly exclaimed "This is not a painting; one walks in this picture"
Work: this equestrian portrait by Jacques-Louis David shows Napoleon on a rearing horse pointing forward, with the names "Hannibal" and "Karolus Magnus" inscribed on rocks
Work: this artist later painted a more historically accurate, subdued version of Napoleon Crossing the Alps, showing him riding a mule instead of a rearing horse
Work: this 2005 parody of the Crossing the Alps composition replaces Napoleon with a modern Black man wearing camouflage fatigues and Timberland boots
back: Napoleon Leading the Army over the Alps (Kehinde Wiley)

front: Work: this Jacques-Louis David painting popularized the "hand-in-waistcoat" pose, showing Napoleon having worked through the night on the Napoleonic Code, indicated by a clock reading 4:13 A.M.
back: The Emperor Napoleon in His Study at the Tuileries (Jacques-Louis David)

front: Work: this Antoine-Jean Gros painting shows Napoleon touching the open sore of a plague victim at a makeshift hospital, inspired by David's Oath of the Horatii
back: Napoleon Visiting the Plague Victims at Jaffa (Antoine-Jean Gros)

front: Work: Antoine-Jean Gros also painted Napoleon at this battle site, visiting soldiers dying in the snow after the battle's aftermath
back: Napoleon on the Battlefield of Eylau (Antoine-Jean Gros)

front: Work: this artist painted Napoleon in red as First Consul and later in ornate ermine coronation robes in Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne
Work: this Antonio Canova sculpture depicts Napoleon nude as the Roman god of war, holding a gilded statue of the goddess Victory
back: Napoleon as Mars the Peacemaker (Antonio Canova)

front: Work: this James Gillray political cartoon depicts Napoleon and British Prime Minister William Pitt carving up a globe
Work: this Rene Magritte painting shows blue sky and clouds painted over a plaster reproduction of Napoleon's death mask
Work: this Jean-Michel Basquiat work depicts Napoleon as a wide-eyed duck alongside a blue star-ribbon
back: Napoleon's Stereotype as Portrayed Circa 1940 (Jean-Michel Basquiat)

front: Work: in this Leo Tolstoy novel, Pierre Bezukhov considers Napoleon "the Antichrist" and calculates his name's numerology to 666 before plotting to assassinate him
Work: the antagonist pig dictator of this George Orwell novel, based on Stalin, is named for Napoleon
Work: the Napoleonic Wars form the backdrop of this William Makepeace Thackeray novel, in which Napoleon is defeated at Waterloo
Work: an alternate-history war against Napoleon is chronicled in this Susanna Clarke novel
Work: Beethoven originally dedicated this symphony to Napoleon, but scratched out his name in fury upon learning he had crowned himself Emperor
Work: in this Stendhal novel, protagonist Fabrizio del Dongo joins Napoleon's Hundred Days campaign
Figure: this admiring French author quipped of Napoleon's writing that "destiny was mute, and Napoleon should have been"
Figure: this philosopher contrasted Napoleon's ascent to power with that of his nephew, writing that history repeats itself "the first time as tragedy, the second time as farce"
Work: this Victor Hugo epic depicts the Battle of Waterloo and the death of Colonel Baron Pontmercy in its "Cosette" volume
Event: in 1803, Napoleon sold this territory to the United States for less than three cents an acre, nearly doubling the young nation's size
work: Family, Legacy & the House of Bonaparte

front: Figure: this brother of Napoleon married American socialite Elizabeth Patterson, a match disapproved of by the family; the American branch of the Bonapartes was based in Baltimore
work: Family, Legacy & the House of Bonaparte

front: Figure: this cavalry general, husband of Napoleon's sister Caroline Bonaparte, was made King of Naples
work: Family, Legacy & the House of Bonaparte

front: Figure: this nephew of Napoleon later seized power in France and founded the Second Empire
work: Family, Legacy & the House of Bonaparte

front: Figure: his name is attached to this term for compensatory behavior attributed to people of short stature
work: Family, Legacy & the House of Bonaparte
